title: Conduct disorder: violating norms in a minor
vignette: A 12-year-old boy is brought to the office due to behavioral problems. His parents are upset about his poor grades and recent school suspension for setting fires in the locker room and stealing another student's cell phone. They say that the boy has always been impulsive, had a lot of energy, and become angry and argumentative easily, but over the past year his aggressive behavior "has gotten out of control." The patient says that the fires were an "accident," although he admits to being angry at the coach for kicking him off the basketball team. Regarding the theft, he jokingly says, "People deserve to have their phones stolen when they are stupid enough to leave them in plain sight." The patient has a history of getting into trouble for talking back to teachers, skipping class, and getting into fights since the fourth grade. He has no medical history. There is a family history of bipolar disorder and alcohol dependence. The patient smokes cigarettes occasionally but denies illicit drug or alcohol use. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

clues.text: setting fires in the locker room and stealing another student's cell phone
clues.note: Fire-setting + theft + aggression are core conduct-disorder behaviors: a repetitive pattern of violating others' rights and major societal norms. Multiple categories (deceit/theft, destruction of property, aggression) push this past oppositional defiance.

clues.text: his aggressive behavior "has gotten out of control
clues.note: Escalating aggression toward people/property over the past year fits the conduct-disorder trajectory in an adolescent. The breadth and severity exceed simple defiance.

clues.text: getting into fights since the fourth grade
clues.note: Onset of rule-violating, aggressive behavior in childhood (since 4th grade, well before age 15) supports conduct disorder and predicts higher risk of progressing to antisocial personality disorder in adulthood.

whatMatters: The diagnosis hinges on the PATTERN and the AGE. A 12-year-old with a year-plus of fire-setting, theft, fighting, and rule-breaking that violates others' rights = conduct disorder. He's a minor, so antisocial personality disorder (≥18) can't apply yet. Answer C.
strategy: Disruptive-behavior ladder: Oppositional defiant disorder = angry/argumentative/defiant but NOT serious rights violations; Conduct disorder = aggression to people/animals, destruction (fire-setting), deceit/theft, serious rule violations in someone <18; Antisocial PD = the same pattern but only diagnosable at ≥18 (with conduct disorder before 15). Match severity + age.

eliminations.label: antisocial PD — can't diagnose under 18 (your pick)
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: antisocial personality disorder is a pervasive pattern of violating others' rights with deceit/aggression. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: by definition it's only diagnosed at age ≥18 (and requires conduct disorder onset before 15); this patient is 12. The childhood version of this picture IS conduct disorder. ✅ Correct if: the patient were ≥18 with a documented history of conduct disorder before age 15.

eliminations.label: ADHD — impulsivity/energy, but not norm violation
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: ADHD features inattention and hyperactivity/impulsivity. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: ADHD alone doesn't include deliberate fire-setting, theft, and rights violations; his impulsivity is a backdrop, but the dominant pattern is conduct disorder (the two can coexist). ✅ Correct if: symptoms were limited to inattention/hyperactivity impairing school without antisocial acts.

eliminations.label: intermittent explosive disorder — impulsive outbursts only
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: IED is recurrent, impulsive aggressive OUTBURSTS out of proportion to provocation, without premeditation or tangible goal. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: his theft and fire-setting are purposeful/instrumental and span multiple norm-violating categories, beyond unplanned rage episodes. ✅ Correct if: the picture were isolated impulsive aggressive outbursts without planned rule-breaking.

eliminations.label: oppositional defiant disorder — defiance without serious rights violations
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: ODD is a pattern of angry/irritable mood, argumentative/defiant behavior, and vindictiveness toward authority. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: ODD does NOT include serious violations of others' rights (aggression to people, property destruction, theft) — those upgrade him to conduct disorder. ✅ Correct if: he were only argumentative/defiant without fire-setting, theft, or fighting.

eliminations.label: pyromania — isolated fire fascination, not this
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: pyromania is deliberate fire-setting for its own sake (tension relief/fascination with fire), without other antisocial behavior. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: his fires are one part of a broad pattern that also includes theft and fighting, and he set the fire out of anger, not fascination. ✅ Correct if: fire-setting were the SOLE problem, driven by fascination/arousal.

reasoning: 12-year-old with a sustained pattern of rights/norm violations
Behaviors span aggression, property destruction (fires), and theft
Onset in childhood (4th grade), too young for antisocial PD (≥18)
Pattern + age = conduct disorder → answer C
mechanism: Conduct disorder is a childhood/adolescent disorder defined by a repetitive pattern of violating major societal norms or the basic rights of others over ~12 months, spanning aggression to people/animals, destruction of property (including fire-setting), deceitfulness/theft, and serious rule violations (truancy, running away). It is more common in boys, presents in middle childhood to adolescence, and increases the risk of progressing to antisocial personality disorder (diagnosable only at ≥18, requiring conduct disorder onset before age 15).

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Psychiatry > Disruptive/conduct disorders: ODD vs conduct disorder vs antisocial PD (age cutoffs and severity), intermittent explosive disorder, pyromania/kleptomania (impulse-control). Memorize the ≥18 rule for antisocial PD and conduct-disorder-before-15 requirement.

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Sort by two axes: severity (defiance vs serious rights violations) and age (<18 vs ≥18). Serious violations in a 12-year-old = conduct disorder.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on conduct disorder, oppositional defiant disorder, antisocial personality disorder, intermittent explosive disorder, pyromania? Tell me which and I'll explain.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: Antisocial PD (A, your pick) is the same behavior pattern but is age-gated to ≥18. At 12, the correct label for that pattern is conduct disorder.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Missed as A → anchor the age rule: under 18 with serious norm violations = conduct disorder; antisocial PD requires age ≥18.

keywords.back: Antisocial personality disorder (needs conduct disorder before 15)
↑ risk of antisocial personality disorder in adulthood
Aggression, property destruction, deceit/theft, serious rule violations

firstAid.detail: Psychiatry: criteria (rights/norm violations >12 months), age of onset, progression to antisocial PD.
firstAid.location: Psychiatry: criteria (rights/norm violations >12 months), age of onset, progression to antisocial PD.

firstAid.detail: Psych: ODD, antisocial PD (≥18), intermittent explosive disorder, pyromania, kleptomania — distinguishing features.
firstAid.location: Psych: ODD, antisocial PD (≥18), intermittent explosive disorder, pyromania, kleptomania — distinguishing features.

firstAid.detail: Psych: ADHD vs disruptive behavior disorders, comorbidity, developmental considerations.
firstAid.location: Psych: ADHD vs disruptive behavior disorders, comorbidity, developmental considerations.

memoryHook: Kid (<18) who violates others' rights (fires + theft + fights) = CONDUCT disorder. Grows up to be antisocial PD (≥18).
